sentences.base_language: I wake up early every morning.
sentences.grammarNote: أَستَيقِظ (astayqiẓ, I wake up) — Form X of root ي-ق-ظ (y-q-ẓ, to be awake): اِستَيقَظَ = to wake up (reflexive/inchoative Form X). مُبَكِّراً (mubakkiran, early) — active participle Form II of ب-ك-ر (b-k-r, early time): used as an adverb in the accusative. كُلَّ صَبَاح (kull ṣabāḥ, every morning) — كُلَّ + indefinite noun, accusative time adverb.

sentences.base_language: I wash my face and get ready for prayer.
sentences.grammarNote: أَغسِل (aghsil, I wash) — Form I of root غ-س-ل (gh-s-l, to wash). وَجهِي (wajhī, my face) — وَجه (face, from root و-ج-ه, to face/direct) + suffix ي (my). أَستَعِدّ (astaʿidd, I prepare/get ready) — Form X of ع-د-د (ʿ-d-d, to count/prepare): اِستَعَدَّ = to get ready. لِلصَّلَاة (liṣ-ṣalāh, for the prayer) — لـ (for) + الصَّلَاة (the prayer, from root ص-ل-و).

sentences.base_language: I pray Fajr — the dawn prayer.
sentences.grammarNote: صلاة الفجر (salat al-fajr) is an إضافة (idafa) — a possessive construct: 'the prayer of dawn'. In Arabic, two nouns are chained directly with no word for 'of': the first noun loses the definite article, the second carries it.

sentences.base_language: After prayer, I sit quietly for a few minutes.
sentences.target_language: بعد الصلاة، أجلس بهدوء لبضع دقائق.
sentences.romanized: ba'd as-salah, ajlis bi-hudu' li-bid' daqa'iq.
sentences.literal: After the-prayer, I-sit in-quiet for-few minutes.
sentences.grammarNote: بَعدَ الصَّلَاة (baʿda aṣ-ṣalāh, after the prayer) — بَعد (after) is a preposition governing the genitive. بِهُدُوء (bi-hudūʾ, quietly) — بـ + هُدُوء (tranquility/calm, from root ه-د-أ, to be calm): a very common adverbial pattern. دَقَائِق (daqāʾiq, minutes) — broken plural of دَقِيقَة (daqīqa, minute): pattern فَعَائِل. لِبِضع (li-bidʿ, for a few) — بِضع means a small number, typically 3–9.

sentences.base_language: Then I go to the kitchen to make tea.
sentences.target_language: ثم أذهب إلى المطبخ لأعمل الشاي.
sentences.romanized: thumma adhhab ila al-matbakh li-a'mal ash-shay.
sentences.literal: Then I-go to the-kitchen to-make the-tea.
sentences.grammarNote: ثُمَّ (thumma, then) — a sequencing conjunction stronger than وَ (and): it implies a clear temporal gap between actions. المَطبَخ (al-maṭbakh, the kitchen) — مَفعَل place-noun from root ط-ب-خ (ṭ-b-kh, to cook): مَطبَخ = place of cooking. لِأَعمَل (li-aʿmal, to make) — لـ (for/in order to) + present-tense verb expressing purpose. الشَّاي (ash-shāy, tea) — a loanword; the definite article الـ assimilates with sun letters.

sentences.base_language: I like tea with fresh mint.
sentences.grammarNote: الطازج (at-tazij) follows النعناع (an-na'na') and agrees with it in definiteness. Arabic adjectives come after the noun and must match it in gender, number, and definiteness — so definite noun + definite adjective.

sentences.base_language: I eat bread with olive oil for breakfast.
sentences.target_language: آكل خبزاً مع زيت الزيتون على الفطور.
sentences.romanized: akul khubzan ma'a zayt az-zaytun 'ala al-futur.
sentences.literal: I-eat bread with oil the-olive on the-breakfast.
sentences.grammarNote: خُبزاً (khubzan, bread) — indefinite accusative (tanwin ـاً) as the direct object. زَيت الزَّيتُون (zayt az-zaytūn, olive oil) is an iḍāfa: زَيت (oil) + الزَّيتُون (the olive). زَيتُون (zaytūn, olive) is a collective noun — it refers to olives as a substance. عَلى الفُطُور (ʿalā al-fuṭūr, for breakfast) — عَلى here has the sense of 'at/with' a meal: a common usage.

sentences.base_language: I get dressed and put on my clothes.
sentences.grammarNote: أَرتَدِي (artadī, I put on/wear) — Form VIII of root ر-د-ي (r-d-y, to don/wear): اِرتَدَى = to dress oneself. مَلَابِسِي (malābisī, my clothes) — مَلَابِس is a broken plural of مَلبَس (clothing), from root ل-ب-س (to wear). أَتَهَيَّأ (atahayya', I prepare/get ready) — Form V of ه-ي-أ (to prepare): a reflexive of preparation. لِلخُرُوج (lil-khurūj, for going out) — خُرُوج is the verbal noun of خَرَجَ (to go out).

sentences.base_language: I check the time — it is six thirty.
sentences.target_language: أتحقق من الوقت — الساعة السادسة والنصف.
sentences.romanized: atahaqqaq min al-waqt — as-sa'a as-sadisa wa-n-nisf.
sentences.literal: I-check from the-time — the-hour the-sixth and-the-half.
sentences.grammarNote: الساعة السادسة والنصف (as-sa'a as-sadisa wa-n-nisf) = six thirty. Arabic tells time using ordinal numbers: السادسة (the sixth) = six o'clock. والنصف (wa-n-nisf) = and the half = thirty minutes past.

sentences.base_language: I say 'bismillah' and leave for work.
sentences.target_language: أقول بسم الله وأغادر إلى العمل.
sentences.grammarNote: بسم الله (bismillah) — 'in the name of God' — is said before beginning almost any action in Arabic-speaking Muslim culture: eating, driving, starting a journey. It reflects the cultural habit of invoking God's blessing at the start of activities.
